subject: Physics / Electronics
description: A comprehensive electronics experiment kit with 25 hands-on experiments covering circuits, LEDs, motors, sensors, solar energy, and more. Students learn fundamental concepts of electricity, magnetism, and energy conversion through practical projects using EVA foam workstations, ring connectors, and push pins.
learning_objectives: Understand basic circuit concepts — open, closed, series, and parallel circuits
Learn about electrical components — LEDs, resistors, transistors, switches, motors
Explore solar energy and energy conversion principles
Understand conductors and insulators through hands-on testing
Learn about electrolysis and chemical effects of electric current
Build practical projects like torch, doorbell, traffic light, and rain alarm
Learn about DC motors and speed control using potentiometers
ncert_connections: Electric Current and Its Effects — Class 7 Chapter 14: Simple circuits, heating and magnetic effects
Chemical Effects of Electric Current — Class 8 Chapter 14: Electrolysis, conductors and insulators
Electricity — Class 10 Chapter 12: Ohm's law, series and parallel circuits, resistance
Magnetic Effects of Electric Current — Class 10 Chapter 13: Motors, electromagnetic induction
Sources of Energy — Class 10 Chapter 14: Solar energy, renewable and non-renewable sources
Some Natural Phenomena — Class 8 Chapter 15: Understanding charge and current flow
Light — Class 8 Chapter 16: Reflection, optical illusion concepts
components: Ring connectors and push pins (for making connections)
Wires (red and black with ring connectors)
Conical cup and aluminium foil (for torch)

troubleshooting.solution: Connect the battery's (+) terminal to the (+) terminal of the component and battery's (-) terminal to their (-) terminal.
Pierce the Resistor ends on the foam sheet through the connector rings.
Pierce Transistor legs (Collector, Base & Emitter) on the foam sheet through the connector rings.

troubleshooting.problem: Connections are right but components not working
troubleshooting.solution: Make sure connector rings touch each other properly and push pins are firmly inserted.

component_basics: The red wire of the AA battery holder is positive (+), and the black wire is negative (-).
The red wire of the 9V battery is positive (+), and the black wire is negative (-).
The red wire of the piezo alarm is positive (+), and the black wire is negative (-).
The long leg of the LED is the positive terminal (+), and the short leg is the negative terminal (-).
A transistor has three legs: Collector (C), Base (B), and Emitter (E). With flat surface on your palm and curved surface up — right leg is Collector, middle is Base, left is Emitter.
precautions: Never short-circuit the terminals of a 9V battery.
Never short-circuit the terminals of a AA battery.
Use the provided foam sheet as a workstation to perform the experiments.
To connect a resistor to wires, pierce ends of resistor through the foam sheet and pass them through the ring connectors.
To connect a resistor to an LED, wrap the resistor around the LED leg.

steps.title: Simple Circuit
steps.detailed_instructions: Build your first simple circuit using an LED and AA battery on the EVA foam workstation. Place one ring connector on top of the other and insert a push pin between them to connect. Connect the positive (red) wire of the battery holder to the long leg (+) of the LED, and the negative (black) wire to the short leg (-) of the LED.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Identify the AA battery holder — red wire is (+), black wire is (-)
Identify the LED — long leg is (+), short leg is (-)
Connect the red (+) wire to the long leg of the LED using ring connectors and push pin
Connect the black (-) wire to the short leg of the LED
Insert the AA battery into the holder — the LED should light up
steps.science_concept: A simple circuit is a closed loop through which electric current flows. It needs a power source (battery), a load (LED), and connecting wires. When the circuit is complete (closed), electrons flow from the negative terminal through the circuit to the positive terminal, lighting the LED. This relates to NCERT Class 7 Chapter 14 — Electric Current and Its Effects.
safety_tips: Don't short-circuit the battery terminals directly
Handle LED gently — legs can break if bent too much

steps.title: LED Circuit with 9V Battery
steps.detailed_instructions: Build an LED circuit using a 9V battery instead of an AA battery. The 9V battery provides higher voltage, making the LED brighter. Use a resistor to protect the LED from burning out due to excess current. Place ring connectors on the foam workstation and use push pins to make connections.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Connect the 9V battery snap connector to the battery
Connect a resistor in series with the LED to limit current
Connect the red (+) wire through the resistor to the LED's long leg (+)
Connect the black (-) wire to the LED's short leg (-)
The LED should glow brightly
steps.science_concept: A 9V battery provides 6 times more voltage than a 1.5V AA battery. Without a resistor, this high voltage would push too much current through the LED and burn it out. The resistor limits current flow — this is an application of Ohm's Law (V = IR). NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity, explains how resistance controls current in a circuit.
safety_tips: Always use a resistor with a 9V battery and LED to prevent burnout
Don't short-circuit the 9V battery terminals

steps.title: Circuit with Switch (Open & Close)
steps.detailed_instructions: Add a switch to your circuit to control when current flows. When the switch is open, the circuit is broken and the LED turns off. When the switch is closed, the circuit is complete and the LED lights up. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins for connections.
sub_steps: Set up a basic LED circuit on the foam workstation
Place the switch in series with the LED and battery
Connect one terminal of the switch to the battery's positive wire
Connect the other terminal of the switch to the LED's positive leg
Complete the circuit by connecting LED's negative leg to battery's negative wire
Toggle the switch ON — LED lights up (closed circuit)
Toggle the switch OFF — LED turns off (open circuit)
steps.science_concept: A switch controls the flow of electric current by opening or closing the circuit. An open circuit has a gap — current cannot flow. A closed circuit is a complete path — current flows freely. This is the fundamental principle behind every electrical switch in your home! NCERT Class 7 Chapter 14 covers how switches work in electric circuits.
safety_tips: Ensure connections are firm before toggling the switch
Don't touch bare wire ends while circuit is on

steps.title: Pencil Circuit
steps.detailed_instructions: Create a circuit using a pencil's graphite core as a conductor. Draw a thick dark line with a pencil on paper, then connect the circuit through the graphite line. The graphite (carbon) in the pencil acts as a resistor — the LED will glow dimly because graphite has higher resistance than metal wires.
sub_steps: Draw a thick, dark pencil line (about 5-8 cm) on a piece of paper
Set up an LED circuit on the foam workstation
Place ring connectors at both ends of the pencil line
Connect one wire from the battery to one end of the pencil line
Connect the other end of the pencil line to the LED
Complete the circuit — the LED should glow dimly
Try making the line thicker or thinner to see the effect on brightness
steps.science_concept: Graphite (pencil lead) is a form of carbon that conducts electricity, but with higher resistance than metals like copper. This experiment demonstrates that different materials have different electrical resistivity. The thicker the graphite line, the lower the resistance, and the brighter the LED glows. This connects to NCERT Class 8 Chapter 14 — Chemical Effects of Electric Current (conductors and insulators).
safety_tips: Use a soft pencil (2B or higher) for better conductivity
Press firmly to make a thick graphite line

steps.title: Series Circuit
steps.detailed_instructions: Connect two or more LEDs in a series circuit where electricity flows through a single route, connecting all components in a line. In a series circuit, if one LED stops working, the entire circuit stops because the path is broken. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Identify two LEDs — note the (+) long leg and (-) short leg on each
Connect the battery's red (+) wire to the first LED's long leg
Connect the first LED's short leg to the second LED's long leg (series link)
Connect the second LED's short leg to the battery's black (-) wire
Both LEDs should light up — now remove one LED to see the other go off too
steps.science_concept: A series circuit has only one path for current flow. The same current passes through every component. If any component breaks or is removed, the entire circuit stops working — just like old Christmas lights! The total resistance in a series circuit is the sum of all individual resistances (R_total = R1 + R2 + ...). NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity explains series and parallel circuits in detail.
safety_tips: Handle LEDs gently while connecting in series
Don't force push pins — they're sharp

steps.title: Parallel Circuit
steps.detailed_instructions: Connect two or more LEDs in a parallel circuit where electricity flows through multiple routes, allowing each component to work independently. If one LED stops working, the others continue to glow. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Identify two LEDs and note their (+) and (-) legs
Connect both LEDs' long legs (+) together to the battery's red (+) wire
Connect both LEDs' short legs (-) together to the battery's black (-) wire
Both LEDs should light up with equal brightness
Remove one LED — the other continues to glow (unlike series circuit)
steps.science_concept: A parallel circuit provides multiple paths for current flow. Each component gets the full battery voltage, so they glow equally bright. If one path breaks, current still flows through the other paths. Your home wiring uses parallel circuits — that's why turning off one light doesn't turn off all others! In parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity.
safety_tips: Ensure correct polarity when connecting LEDs in parallel
Don't connect too many LEDs to a single battery

steps.title: Simple Fan
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a simple fan using a DC motor, propeller, and 9V battery. Use cutout 1 from the booklet to make a fan base. Connect the motor directly to the battery — the propeller will spin, creating airflow like a fan.
sub_steps: Assemble the fan base using cutout 1 from the cutout booklet
Attach the propeller to the DC motor shaft
Place the motor on the fan base
Connect the red (+) wire of the battery to one terminal of the DC motor
Connect the black (-) wire to the other terminal of the motor
The propeller should spin — you've made a simple fan!
steps.science_concept: A DC motor converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotation). Inside the motor, a coil of wire sits in a magnetic field. When current flows through the coil, it experiences a force (Fleming's Left Hand Rule) that causes it to rotate. The propeller attached to the motor shaft then moves air. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 13 — Magnetic Effects of Electric Current.
safety_tips: Keep fingers away from the spinning propeller
Don't touch motor terminals while connected to battery

steps.title: Reversing Propeller Spin
steps.detailed_instructions: Learn how to reverse the direction of a DC motor by swapping the battery connections. Connect the battery terminals to the opposite terminals of the motor to change its rotational direction. The propeller will spin in the opposite direction.
sub_steps: Build the simple fan from Experiment 7
Note which direction the propeller is spinning
Disconnect the wires from the motor
Swap the connections — connect red wire to where black was, and vice versa
Connect to the battery again — the propeller now spins in the opposite direction!
Try switching back and forth to observe the direction change
steps.science_concept: The direction of a DC motor's rotation depends on the direction of current flow through the coil. When you reverse the polarity (swap + and - connections), the current flows in the opposite direction, and the force on the coil reverses — so the motor spins the other way. This is based on Fleming's Left Hand Rule. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 13 — Magnetic Effects of Electric Current.
safety_tips: Always disconnect battery before swapping wires
Keep fingers away from spinning propeller

steps.title: Fan with Switch
steps.detailed_instructions: Add a switch to your fan circuit to control when the motor spins. Place the switch in series with the DC motor and battery. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins for connections. Toggle the switch to turn the fan on and off.
sub_steps: Build the simple fan circuit on the EVA foam workstation
Place the switch in series between the battery and motor
Connect battery (+) to one terminal of the switch
Connect the other terminal of the switch to the motor
Connect the motor's other terminal to battery (-)
Use ring connectors and push pins for firm connections
Toggle the switch to turn the fan ON and OFF
steps.science_concept: This combines the switch concept (Experiment 3) with the motor concept (Experiment 7). The switch acts as a gate — when open, no current flows and the motor is off. When closed, current flows and the motor spins. Every fan in your home uses this same principle! NCERT Class 7 Chapter 14 — Electric Current and Its Effects.
safety_tips: Ensure firm connections to prevent sparking
Keep fingers away from propeller when switching on

steps.title: Speed Controller
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a speed controller for your DC motor using a potentiometer (variable resistor). The potentiometer lets you adjust the resistance in the circuit, which changes the current flowing to the motor and thus controls its speed. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins.
sub_steps: Build the fan circuit on the EVA foam workstation
Place the potentiometer in series with the motor and 9V battery
Connect one end terminal of the potentiometer to the battery's (+) wire
Connect the middle (wiper) terminal to the motor
Connect the motor's other terminal to the battery's (-) wire
Use ring connectors and push pins for connections
Turn the potentiometer knob slowly — watch the motor speed change!
steps.science_concept: A potentiometer is a variable resistor — turning its knob changes the resistance in the circuit. Higher resistance means less current flows (Ohm's Law: I = V/R), so the motor spins slower. Lower resistance means more current and faster spinning. This is how ceiling fan regulators and light dimmers work! NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity (variable resistance and Ohm's law).
safety_tips: Turn the potentiometer slowly to avoid sudden speed changes
Don't set resistance to zero — it may damage the motor with too much current

steps.title: Touch Sensor LED
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a touch-sensitive LED circuit using a transistor (NPN). When you touch the base and collector legs of the transistor simultaneously with your finger, your body's small electrical resistance completes the circuit and the LED lights up. Refer to the transistor identification notes to correctly identify Collector, Base, and Emitter legs.
sub_steps: Identify the transistor legs: Collector (C) = right, Base (B) = middle, Emitter (E) = left (flat surface facing away)
Pierce the transistor legs through the foam sheet
Connect the LED's long leg (+) to the Collector (C) of the transistor through a resistor
Connect battery (+) to the resistor/LED circuit
Connect the Emitter (E) to the battery (-)
Leave the Base (B) exposed for touching
Touch the Base and Collector simultaneously with your finger — the LED lights up!
steps.science_concept: A transistor can act as an electronic switch. The NPN transistor has three terminals: Collector (C), Base (B), and Emitter (E). When a small current flows into the Base, it allows a much larger current to flow from Collector to Emitter — this is called amplification. Your finger provides enough resistance to let a tiny current flow into the Base, switching on the LED. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 13 (advanced concept).
safety_tips: Handle the transistor carefully — legs are fragile
Refer to transistor identification page before connecting
Make sure your hands are dry for consistent results

steps.title: Touch Sensor Alarm
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a touch-activated alarm using a transistor and piezo buzzer. Similar to the Touch Sensor LED experiment, but replace the LED with a piezo alarm. When you touch the transistor's Base and Collector, the alarm sounds. Refer to the transistor identification notes.
sub_steps: Identify the transistor legs: C (right), B (middle), E (left)
Pierce the transistor legs through the foam sheet
Connect the piezo alarm's red (+) wire to the Collector (C)
Connect battery (+) to the piezo alarm circuit
Connect the Emitter (E) to the battery (-)
Connect the piezo alarm's black (-) wire appropriately in the circuit
Touch Base and Collector simultaneously — the alarm sounds!
steps.science_concept: This extends the transistor switch concept to power a piezo buzzer instead of an LED. A piezo buzzer contains a piezoelectric crystal that vibrates when electricity passes through it, producing sound. The transistor acts as a touch-activated switch — no mechanical parts needed! This is the basic principle behind modern touchscreens and touch-sensitive devices. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 13.
safety_tips: The piezo alarm can be loud — don't hold it close to your ear
Refer to transistor identification page before connecting

steps.title: LED-Alarm Dual Connection Play
steps.detailed_instructions: Create a circuit that connects both an LED and a piezo alarm together. Use ring connectors and push pins on the EVA foam workstation. This experiment demonstrates how multiple components can be connected in a single circuit to perform different functions simultaneously.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Set up the LED circuit with appropriate resistor
Add the piezo alarm in parallel with the LED
Connect both components to the battery through ring connectors
Use push pins to secure all connections on the foam sheet
Close the circuit — both the LED lights up and the alarm sounds simultaneously
steps.science_concept: This experiment combines what you learned about LEDs and piezo alarms in a single circuit. When connected in parallel, each component gets the full battery voltage and works independently. The LED converts electrical energy to light, while the piezo buzzer converts it to sound — both are forms of energy conversion from electrical energy. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity (parallel circuits).
safety_tips: Use a resistor with the LED to protect it
The alarm can be loud — be prepared

steps.title: Electrolysis
steps.detailed_instructions: Perform electrolysis using pencils as electrodes in salt water. Chlorine gas is released at the pencil connected to the battery's positive (+) terminal, and hydrogen gas is released at the pencil connected to the negative (-) terminal. You'll see bubbles forming at both pencils.
sub_steps: Fill a container with salt water (dissolve a spoonful of salt in water)
Sharpen two pencils at both ends to expose the graphite core
Dip the sharpened ends of both pencils into the salt water
Connect one pencil to the battery's positive (+) terminal using alligator clips or wires
Connect the other pencil to the battery's negative (-) terminal
Observe bubbles forming at both pencils — these are gases!
The pencil at (+) releases chlorine gas, the pencil at (-) releases hydrogen gas
steps.science_concept: Electrolysis is the process of using electric current to drive a chemical reaction. When current passes through salt water (NaCl solution), it breaks down into its chemical components. At the positive electrode (anode), chlorine gas (Cl₂) is released. At the negative electrode (cathode), hydrogen gas (H₂) is released. The graphite in pencils acts as inert electrodes. NCERT Class 8 Chapter 14 — Chemical Effects of Electric Current.
safety_tips: Perform this experiment in a well-ventilated area — chlorine gas has a sharp smell
Don't inhale the gases directly
Use salt water, not plain water, for better conductivity
Adult supervision recommended

steps.title: Solar Powered Light
steps.detailed_instructions: Use the solar panel to power an LED without any battery! Place the solar panel in sunlight and connect it to an LED using ring connectors and push pins on the EVA foam workstation. Use cutout 4 to make a solar panel stand.
sub_steps: Assemble the solar panel stand using cutout 4
Place the solar panel on the stand, angled toward sunlight
Connect the solar panel's positive (+) wire to the LED's long leg (+)
Connect the solar panel's negative (-) wire to the LED's short leg (-)
Use ring connectors and push pins on the foam workstation
Place the setup in direct sunlight — the LED should light up!
Try shading the panel with your hand to see the LED dim or turn off
steps.science_concept: Solar panels convert light energy directly into electrical energy using the photovoltaic effect. When photons (light particles) hit the solar cell, they knock electrons loose from silicon atoms, creating an electric current. This is a clean, renewable source of energy! The sun provides about 1000 watts per square meter of energy. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 14 — Sources of Energy.
safety_tips: Don't look directly at the sun while positioning the solar panel
Handle the solar panel carefully — it can be fragile

steps.title: The Energy Bridge
steps.detailed_instructions: Test different materials to determine which are conductors (allow electricity to pass) and which are insulators (block electricity). Place different objects like pencil nibs, salt water, rubber, plastic, spoons, and iron between the ring connectors. If the LED lights up, the material is a conductor. Use cutout 4 for the solar panel stand and a piece of cardboard.
sub_steps: Set up an LED circuit with a gap between two ring connectors
The gap is your 'energy bridge' where you'll test materials
Place a metal spoon between the connectors — LED lights up (conductor!)
Replace with a rubber band — LED stays off (insulator!)
Test pencil graphite — LED glows dimly (weak conductor / resistor)
Test salt water — LED lights up (conductor due to dissolved ions)
Test plastic, paper, wood, iron nail, and other objects
Make a table of conductors vs insulators from your results
steps.science_concept: Materials that allow electric current to pass through them easily are called conductors (metals, salt water, graphite). Materials that resist the flow of current are called insulators (rubber, plastic, wood, paper). Some materials are in between — they conduct partially and are called semiconductors. NCERT Class 8 Chapter 14 — Chemical Effects of Electric Current covers conductors and insulators.
safety_tips: Don't test materials connected to household electricity
Be careful with salt water near electronic components
Wipe ring connectors clean between tests

steps.title: Solar Windmill
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a solar-powered windmill (fan) by connecting the solar panel to a DC motor with a propeller. Place in sunlight and watch the propeller spin without any battery! Use cutout 1 for the fan base and cutout 4 for the solar panel stand. Use ring connectors and push pins on the EVA foam workstation.
sub_steps: Assemble the fan base using cutout 1 from the cutout booklet
Assemble the solar panel stand using cutout 4
Attach the propeller to the DC motor shaft
Place the motor on the fan base
Connect the solar panel's wires to the DC motor terminals
Use ring connectors and push pins on the foam workstation
Place the solar panel in direct sunlight — the propeller will spin!
steps.science_concept: This experiment demonstrates double energy conversion: Solar energy → Electrical energy (via solar panel) → Mechanical energy (via DC motor). The solar panel generates electricity from sunlight, which drives the motor to spin the propeller. Real windmills work the opposite way — wind (mechanical) drives a generator to produce electricity! NCERT Class 10 Chapter 14 — Sources of Energy.
safety_tips: Keep fingers away from spinning propeller
Handle solar panel carefully

steps.title: Solar Optical Illusion
steps.detailed_instructions: Create an optical illusion using solar-powered spinning. Connect the solar panel to a DC motor and attach a disc with patterns drawn on it. When the disc spins fast in sunlight, your eyes perceive an illusion due to persistence of vision. Use cutout 4 for the solar panel stand.
sub_steps: Assemble the solar panel stand using cutout 4
Connect the solar panel to the DC motor
Create or use a patterned disc (e.g., with black and white segments)
Attach the disc to the motor shaft
Place the solar panel in direct sunlight to spin the disc
Observe the optical illusion as the disc spins — patterns blend and merge!
Try different patterns to create different illusions
steps.science_concept: This experiment demonstrates persistence of vision — your eyes retain an image for about 1/16th of a second after it disappears. When the patterned disc spins fast enough, your brain blends consecutive images together, creating the illusion of movement or merged patterns. This is the same principle behind movies and TV — they show 24-30 frames per second, and your brain perceives smooth motion! NCERT Class 8 Chapter 16 — Light.
safety_tips: Don't stare at the spinning disc for too long
Keep fingers away from the spinning motor shaft

steps.title: Home Supply System
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a miniature home electrical supply system with an LED (representing a light) and a DC motor with propeller (representing a fan), each controlled by its own switch. This simulates how your home's electrical wiring works — each appliance has its own independent switch. Use the EVA foam workstation.
sub_steps: Set up the EVA foam workstation
Connect an LED with a resistor to one switch (this is your 'room light')
Connect a DC motor with propeller to another switch (this is your 'ceiling fan')
Connect both circuits in parallel to the 9V battery
Toggle switch 1 — the LED (light) turns on/off independently
Toggle switch 2 — the motor (fan) turns on/off independently
Both can work simultaneously or independently — just like home wiring!
steps.science_concept: Home electrical wiring uses parallel circuits — each room/appliance gets the same voltage from the main supply and has its own switch for independent control. If one appliance fails, others keep working. This experiment demonstrates this parallel wiring concept on a small scale. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity (domestic electric circuits, parallel connections).
safety_tips: Keep connections firm to prevent short circuits
Don't touch motor while running

steps.title: Traffic Light
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a traffic light system using three LEDs (representing red, yellow, and green signals) with resistors and switches. Each LED is controlled by its own switch, simulating how traffic lights change. Use the EVA foam workstation with ring connectors and push pins.
sub_steps: Set up the EVA foam workstation
Connect three LEDs (red, yellow, green) each with its own resistor
Connect each LED-resistor pair to its own switch
Wire all three circuits in parallel to the 9V battery
Toggle switches to simulate traffic light sequence:
- Red ON (others OFF) → Stop
- Yellow ON (others OFF) → Get Ready
- Green ON (others OFF) → Go
Practice the traffic light sequence!
steps.science_concept: This experiment combines parallel circuits with multiple switches to create a practical application. Each LED circuit is independent — controlled by its own switch. Real traffic lights use timers and microcontrollers to automatically switch between lights. The different colors are produced by different semiconductor materials in the LEDs. NCERT Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity (parallel circuits).
safety_tips: Always use resistors with LEDs to prevent burnout
Ensure correct polarity for each LED

steps.title: Rain Alarm
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a rain alarm that sounds a piezo buzzer when water bridges two exposed wire contacts. Use salt water to simulate rain. The water acts as a conductor between two open contacts, completing the circuit and triggering the alarm. Use alligator clips and secure wires with tape.
sub_steps: Connect the 9V battery to the piezo alarm through two separated wire contacts
Leave a gap between the two contacts (open circuit — alarm is silent)
Use alligator clips for secure connections
Secure the wire with tape where needed
Place the exposed contacts where rain would fall
Drop salt water on the contacts — water bridges the gap
The alarm sounds! Water completes the circuit as a conductor
steps.science_concept: Water (especially salt water) is an electrical conductor because dissolved salts create ions that carry electric charge. When rainwater (which contains dissolved minerals) bridges two exposed contacts, it completes the circuit, allowing current to flow and the alarm to sound. Pure distilled water is actually a poor conductor — it's the dissolved ions that conduct! NCERT Class 8 Chapter 14 — Chemical Effects of Electric Current.
safety_tips: Keep the battery and buzzer dry — only the sensor contacts should get wet
Use salt water for reliable demonstration
Adult supervision recommended when using water near electronics

steps.title: Tornado
steps.detailed_instructions: Create a mini tornado (vortex) in a glass of water using a DC motor with propeller. Hold the motor above a glass of water so the spinning propeller creates a whirlpool effect. The spinning motion creates a vortex that looks like a tornado.
sub_steps: Fill a clear glass or transparent container with water
Attach the propeller to the DC motor
Connect the DC motor to the 9V battery
Hold the motor above the water surface with the propeller just touching the water
Switch on the motor — the spinning propeller creates a vortex in the water
Observe the tornado-like whirlpool forming in the center!
steps.science_concept: The spinning propeller creates a rotational force in the water, forming a vortex — a spinning column of fluid. The center of the vortex has lower pressure than the surrounding water, which is the same principle behind real tornadoes! Tornadoes form when rotating air creates a low-pressure zone, pulling in surrounding air. This demonstrates how energy conversion (electrical → mechanical → fluid motion) creates natural phenomena. NCERT Class 9 — Force and Laws of Motion (circular motion concepts).
safety_tips: Hold the motor firmly — it can vibrate while spinning
Don't let the motor fall into the water
Keep battery and connections away from water
Adult supervision recommended
tools_needed: Clear glass or container with water

steps.title: DIY Torch
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a working flashlight/torch from scratch using a cardboard cylinder, aluminium foil cone reflector, LED, switch, and AA battery. Roll cutout 3 into a cylinder, create a reflective cone from a conical cup lined with aluminium foil, and assemble the circuit inside.
sub_steps: Use cutout 3 and roll it into a cylinder (13cm x 3cm dimensions)
Cut a conical cup top and wrap aluminium foil inside it to create a reflector
Attach the cone to one end of the cylinder using strong adhesive
Build the LED circuit: connect AA battery to LED with a switch
Place the circuit inside the cylindrical torch case
Pierce holes for wires connected to the switch at the base
Paste the circle cutout (3.8cm) at the bottom of the cylinder
Press the switch to activate the torch — it works!
steps.science_concept: A torch combines several science concepts: electrical circuits (battery + LED + switch), reflection of light (aluminium foil cone focuses the LED light forward), and energy conversion (chemical energy in battery → electrical energy → light energy). The cone-shaped reflector uses the principle of reflection to concentrate light into a beam. NCERT Class 8 Chapter 16 — Light (reflection) and Class 10 Chapter 12 — Electricity.
safety_tips: Use scissors carefully when cutting cardboard
Apply adhesive in a well-ventilated area
Handle the sharp edges of cut aluminium foil carefully
Adult supervision recommended for cutting

steps.title: Door Bell
steps.detailed_instructions: Build a working doorbell using a piezo alarm/buzzer and a switch. Press the switch to complete the circuit and ring the doorbell. This simulates a real doorbell where pressing the button temporarily completes the circuit. Use the EVA foam workstation.
sub_steps: Place the EVA foam workstation on a flat surface
Connect the piezo alarm's red (+) wire to the switch
Connect the other terminal of the switch to the battery's (+) terminal
Connect the piezo alarm's black (-) wire to the battery's (-) terminal
Mount the switch in an accessible position
Press the switch to ring the doorbell!
Release the switch — the bell stops (momentary contact)
steps.science_concept: A doorbell uses a momentary switch — it completes the circuit only while you press it. The piezo buzzer inside vibrates using the piezoelectric effect: certain crystals produce vibrations when voltage is applied, creating sound waves. Modern doorbells may be wireless, but the basic principle of completing a circuit to activate a sound device remains the same. NCERT Class 7 Chapter 14 — Electric Current and Its Effects.
safety_tips: The piezo alarm can be surprisingly loud
Don't hold the buzzer directly against your ear

steps.title: DIY Aluminium Switch
steps.detailed_instructions: Create your own switch using an aluminium strip! Place the aluminium strip between the open ends of the black wire to act as a switch. When the aluminium strip connects both wire ends, the circuit is complete and the LED lights up. Remove or lift the strip to break the circuit. Use the EVA foam workstation.
sub_steps: Set up an LED circuit on the EVA foam workstation with a gap in the wire
Cut a small strip of aluminium foil
Place the aluminium strip between the open (disconnected) ends of the wire
When the aluminium touches both wire ends — the LED lights up (circuit closed)
Lift the aluminium strip — LED turns off (circuit open)
You've made your own DIY switch using a conductor!
Try other conducting materials to see if they also work as switches
steps.science_concept: A switch is simply a device that can connect (close) or disconnect (open) a circuit. Aluminium is an excellent conductor of electricity — when it bridges two wire ends, current flows through it to complete the circuit. This experiment shows that any conductor can be used as a switch. Real switches use metallic contacts that physically touch or separate. NCERT Class 7 Chapter 14 — Electric Current and Its Effects.
safety_tips: Aluminium foil edges can be sharp — handle carefully
Don't use very thin foil that might tear easily
